accessibility_guidance.registry_rule: Complex charts, diagrams, maps, and infographics need a short text alternative plus a longer equivalent description.
Short alternatives may point readers to a programmatically associated or nearby full text description when the figure carries complex content.
Color must not be the only visual channel for meaning; labels, position, shape, text, or another cue must preserve the distinction.
accessibility_guidance.source: WCAG 2.2 Understanding SC 1.4.1 Use of Color

accessibility_guidance.registry_rule: Meaningful graphical objects and visual cues need enough contrast to be perceived without relying on low-contrast marks.
Complex figure text should identify the chart or diagram type, purpose, important relationships, and highlighted evidence.
Data visuals should prefer simple common forms, direct labels, limited concept load, and lossless textual equivalents.
accessibility_guidance.source: U.S. Web Design System Data Visualizations

accessibility_guidance.registry_rule: Figures must not rely on color alone; rendered text, symbols, labels, and contrast should preserve meaning for readers who do not perceive the palette.
Rendered PDF figures must be checked as figures with useful alternative text, not only as source Markdown rows.

If authority is present it proceeds to Targeting Scope, then to a Minimization Filter that applies relevance and protected-person rules. The filter branches: pertinent data goes to Retention and Analysis, while non-pertinent data goes to Purge. Both Retention and Purge feed a Compliance Audit node that loops back to the Legal Authority Check.
figures.caption: This diagram teaches how a lawful-authority check and minimization rules gate signals collection so that non-pertinent and protected data are filtered before retention. It is anchored to the signals intelligence sigint / sigint fundamentals section; use it to inspect Collection Request, Minimization Filter, and Compliance Audit while preserving the distinction between curriculum structure, evidence boundary, and accountable practice.

figures.alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart of cryptographic assurance. A Protected Message passes through four assurance layers shown as parallel branches: Confidentiality via encryption, Integrity via hashing, Authentication via digital signatures, and Provenance via key management and certificates. Each branch feeds a Verification node, and all Verification results converge on an Assurance Decision that either accepts a trusted message or flags it for review. The decision loops back to a Policy and Key Lifecycle node governing the layers.
figures.caption: This diagram teaches the layered properties that together provide cryptographic assurance: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and verifiable provenance. Its reader value is to make Protected Message, Verification Checks, and Policy and Key Lifecycle visible at a glance, with the signals intelligence sigint / modern sigint and cryptography section as the source section and defensive review as the boundary.

figures.alt_text: A top-to-bottom governance loop. Perception feeds belief update, which feeds intent formation. Intent passes through a policy and tool-allowlist gate. A risk classifier branches to either a human-in-the-loop review gate for high-risk actions or a bounded action executor for low-risk actions. The human gate also reaches the executor. The executor sends results to an observability log, which feeds both a kill-switch loop detector and the human review gate, and loops back to perception. The kill-switch can halt the executor.
figures.caption: This diagram shows how an agentic intelligence system is bounded by least-privilege tooling, human review gates, observability, and kill-switch stop conditions before any action takes effect. figures.alt_text: A workflow. Frame the analytic question feeds enumeration of a full set of competing hypotheses and, in parallel, collection of evidence and assumptions. A sensitivity check tests reliance on key items: if findings are not robust, it loops back to revisit hypotheses; if robust, the analyst reports the conclusion with confidence levels and flags indicators to watch.

figures.alt_text: Flowchart. A National Policy and Doctrine node leads to a Statutory Legal Authority node, which leads to an Authorization and Tasking node. That node connects to an Oversight and Compliance Review node. Three governance inputs feed the oversight review: Interagency Coordination, Rules of Engagement and Law of Armed Conflict, and Civilian Oversight Bodies. The oversight review leads to an Approval Gate diamond. The approval gate has two outputs: an Approved Planning Support node and a Halt and Document Objection node. Approved Planning Support leads to a Records and Audit Trail node, which leads back to Civilian Oversight Bodies to close the loop.
figures.caption: How intelligence support to special operations flows only through documented legal authority, oversight review, and approval gates before any approved planning proceeds. figures.alt_text: Flowchart timeline of organizations. Cheka 1917 leads to OGPU 1923, which leads to NKVD 1934, which leads to MGB and MVD late 1940s, which leads to KGB 1954. The KGB 1954 node branches at the 1991 dissolution into three successor nodes: SVR Foreign Intelligence, FSB Federal Security Service, and FSO Federal Protective Service. A separate Military Intelligence GRU node, founded 1918, runs parallel and connects to a present-day Modern Russian Services node, which also receives SVR, FSB, and FSO. A Declassified Record and Provenance Caveat node annotates the whole chart.
figures.caption: A historical organizational chart tracing the institutional lineage of Soviet and Russian state security from the Cheka to its modern successor services, studied from the declassified record. In the historical intelligence services / soviet and russian intelligence section, it lets readers compare Cheka, 1917, OGPU, 1923, NKVD, 1934, and MGB and MVD, late 1940s so the visual functions as a traceable course aid rather than an unscoped assertion.

figures.alt_text: Flowchart. A BRUSA Agreement 1943 node leads to a UKUSA Agreement 1946 to 1956 node, which is the central governance hub. The UKUSA hub connects to five partner nodes: United States NSA, United Kingdom GCHQ, Canada CSE, Australia ASD, and New Zealand GCSB. The UKUSA hub also connects to three governance-rule nodes: Shared Handling and Marking Rules, Legal Authority per Nation, and Source-Protection Caveats. All three governance-rule nodes feed a National Oversight Bodies node. A Declassified 2010 Release Provenance node annotates the UKUSA hub.

figures.alt_text: Flowchart of a framework for analyzing a historical ICS cyber incident. Select a documented public incident, then gather verified open sources with provenance. A reviewer check for overclaiming and attribution caution either loops back to sources or proceeds to mapping the lesson to a durable defensive control and a tabletop inject, ending with a recorded refresh trigger.

figures.alt_text: Flowchart of a defensive assurance loop for AGEINT applied to ICS. Synthetic OT telemetry from an owned lab feeds AI-driven anomaly detection and an LLM agent for log analysis and threat correlation. Both produce advisory findings with confidence, which pass an allowlist and tool-governance gate. An analyst-in-the-loop review either rejects unsafe automation back to the lab or approves a logged defensive recommendation. The recommendation goes to a tabletop debrief with rollback evidence, recording coverage and a refresh trigger that loops back to the telemetry source.

figures.alt_text: Flowchart of an ethics and oversight decision framework. A proposed activity enters and is tested against four ethical lenses: deontological duties and rights, consequentialist harm and benefit, virtue and integrity, and proportionality versus a less intrusive means. These feed an authority check asking whether lawful or classroom authority exists, which can stop the activity or proceed. Next an oversight and audit-trail check can require independent review or proceed to confirming a redress path for affected people, ending in an approved bounded activity recorded in an authority-and-impact register with a review date; failure of any gate routes to stop and document.
figures.caption: A governance gate that tests a proposed activity for authority, proportionality, rights, oversight, and redress before any work may proceed. Its reader value is to make Proposed activity, Deontological duties and rights lens, Consequentialist harm and benefit lens, and Virtue and integrity lens visible at a glance, with the legal ethical and oversight frameworks / ethics of intelligence and cognitive security section as the source section and defensive review as the boundary.

figures.alt_text: Square system atlas for AGEINT. A dark source spine anchors locked reference keys and verified sources at the bottom. Discipline lanes for HUMINT, SIGINT, OSINT, GEOINT, FININT, cyber, cognitive security, and governance feed a central Synthetic Analytic Tradecraft core. Above it, bounded agentic assistance can retrieve, reason, structure, and hand off, but cannot approve external action. Around the system, verification and safety gates check citations, figures, metadata, PDF links, rights, and human review before any product is trusted.
figures.caption: The Synthetic Tradecraft System Atlas replaces the former graphical abstract with a governed-system view of AGEINT. It shows the source spine as the evidence floor; intelligence disciplines as collection and context lanes; Synthetic Analytic Tradecraft as the central claim-making contract; bounded agentic assistance as a limited support layer; and verification, safety, and human-review gates as the outer assurance ring. The figure is a curriculum architecture map, not a claim that AGEINT has measured field capability or learning outcomes.
